course.title: Data Structures
course.description: This course covers fundamental and advanced data structures with real-world applications. Students will learn to design, analyze, and implement efficient data structures for solving complex computational problems relevant to modern software engineering.

modules.title: Lists
modules.description: Covers Abstract Data Types, List ADT, and various linked list implementations along with applications.

chapters.title: Abstract Data Types and List ADT
chapters.description: Introduction to ADTs, their significance, and the List ADT with its operations and interface.

lessons.title: Introduction to Abstract Data Types (ADTs)
lessons.description: Understanding what ADTs are, why they matter, and how they differ from data structures.

topics.title: What is an Abstract Data Type?

subtopics.title: Definition and Concept of ADT
subtopics.content:
An Abstract Data Type (ADT) is a theoretical concept in computer science that defines a data type purely by its behavior (semantics) from the user's perspective — specifically, the possible values it can hold and the operations that can be performed on it — without specifying the concrete implementation details. The word 'abstract' means we are concerned only with WHAT the data type does, not HOW it does it internally.

Think of an ADT as a contract or an interface: it tells you what you can do with the data, but completely hides the internal mechanics. For example, a 'Stack' ADT may declare: you can push elements, pop elements, and peek at the top. But whether the stack is physically backed by a Python list, a linked list of nodes, or a fixed array — that is irrelevant to the user of the ADT.

This concept of abstraction is one of the core pillars of software engineering. It allows developers to reason about programs at a high level, separate design concerns from implementation concerns, and swap out implementations for better-performing ones without breaking any existing code that uses the ADT.

Formally, an ADT is defined by three components: (1) A domain — the set of values the type can represent (e.g., a list of integers); (2) A set of operations — the functions applicable to those values (e.g., insert, delete, find); and (3) A set of axioms or contracts — rules describing how operations must behave (e.g., 'after push(x), peek() must return x'). This formal definition makes ADTs a powerful tool for both designing systems and proving their correctness mathematically.
examples:
# A BankAccount ADT — the user doesn't know if balance is in RAM, DB, or file
class BankAccountADT:
    def deposit(self, amount): pass
    def withdraw(self, amount): pass
    def get_balance(self): pass
    def get_statement(self): pass

# Usage — completely independent of how data is stored
account = BankAccountADT()
account.deposit(5000)
account.withdraw(1500)
print(account.get_balance())  # 3500

# Python's own built-in types are ADTs
# list ADT operations: append, pop, insert, remove, index
my_list = []
my_list.append(10)     # insert at end
my_list.insert(0, 5)   # insert at position
my_list.pop()          # remove from end
print(my_list.index(5)) # find by value
# You never worry about whether CPython uses a dynamic array internally!

subtopics.title: ADT vs Data Structure
subtopics.content:
Students often confuse ADTs with data structures, but these are two distinct concepts operating at fundamentally different levels of abstraction. An ADT is a logical description — it defines what a type is and what operations it supports. A Data Structure is the actual, concrete implementation of that ADT in memory — it defines how data is physically organized and how operations are executed in code.

The relationship between an ADT and a data structure is best understood through an analogy: an ADT is like an architectural blueprint that specifies 'the building will have 3 bedrooms and 2 bathrooms,' while the data structure is the actual constructed building with specific bricks, beams, wiring, and plumbing. The blueprint doesn't tell you what materials to use — that's the builder's job.

For example, the 'List ADT' defines operations like insert, delete, and access. This single ADT can be realized as an Array-based List (contiguous memory) or as a Linked List (nodes connected by pointers). Both are valid data structures implementing the same ADT contract, but they have vastly different performance profiles: array lists offer O(1) random access but O(n) insertion at the middle, while linked lists offer O(1) insertion at head but O(n) random access.

This distinction is foundational in software design because it enables polymorphism and substitutability. You can write code that depends only on the ADT interface, and later plug in a different data structure for better performance — without modifying any of the client code. This is exactly what Python's abstract base classes (ABC module) and Java's interfaces formalize.
examples:
# ADT: Map/Dictionary
# Same interface, multiple data structure implementations:
# 1. Python dict — hash table implementation
hash_map = {}
hash_map['name'] = 'Alice'
print(hash_map['name'])

# 2. Sorted dict (tree-based) via sortedcontainers
# from sortedcontainers import SortedDict
# tree_map = SortedDict()
# tree_map['name'] = 'Alice'
# Same interface, different internal structure!

# Comparison table in code comments:
# | Operation  | Array List | Linked List |
# |------------|------------|-------------|
# | Access [i] |   O(1)     |    O(n)     |
# | Insert mid |   O(n)     |    O(1)*    |
# | Delete mid |   O(n)     |    O(1)*    |
# | Search     |   O(n)     |    O(n)     |
# (* given pointer to node)
# Same ADT, drastically different performance!

subtopics.title: Why ADTs Matter in Modern Software Engineering
subtopics.content:
ADTs are not just academic constructs — they are the invisible backbone of modern software engineering. Virtually every major system, library, and framework you use daily is built on ADT principles. Understanding ADTs deeply transforms how you design software: instead of thinking about memory layouts and pointer arithmetic, you think in terms of behaviors, contracts, and interfaces.

One of the most powerful benefits of ADTs is modular design and code reusability. A function written to operate on a 'List ADT' works correctly for any list implementation — array, linked, skip list, etc. This means you write the algorithm once and it works everywhere the contract is satisfied. Python's sort() method, for instance, works on any sequence that implements comparison operations — it doesn't know or care if it's sorting a Python list, a NumPy array, or a custom collection.

ADTs also dramatically improve software maintainability. When an internal implementation needs to change (perhaps for performance), only the implementation file changes — the rest of the codebase, which only depends on the ADT interface, remains completely unaffected. This is the principle behind the open/closed principle in SOLID design: open for extension, closed for modification.

In professional settings, ADTs form the basis of API design. Companies like Google, Amazon, and Meta publish stable APIs for their services — these APIs are ADTs: they define what operations are available and what they do, without revealing how the servers actually process requests. Engineers who understand ADTs write better APIs that last years without breaking, because they know to expose behavior, not implementation.
examples:
# Python's collections.abc module defines ADTs as abstract base classes
from collections.abc import MutableSequence

# MutableSequence is the ADT — defines insert, __getitem__, __setitem__, __delitem__, __len__
# list, bytearray, and custom classes are data structures that implement it

class MyCustomList(MutableSequence):
    # Implement the ADT contract
    def __init__(self):
        self._store = []
    def __getitem__(self, i): return self._store[i]
    def __setitem__(self, i, v): self._store[i] = v
    def __delitem__(self, i): del self._store[i]
    def __len__(self): return len(self._store)
    def insert(self, i, v): self._store.insert(i, v)

# Now built-in methods work on our custom list!
cl = MyCustomList()
cl.append(1); cl.append(2); cl.append(3)
print(list(reversed(cl)))  # Works! [3, 2, 1]

# Real-world: Linux VFS (Virtual File System) as an ADT
# Every filesystem (ext4, FAT32, NTFS) implements the same file ADT:
# open(), read(), write(), close(), seek()
# Python's file ADT works the same way:
with open('data.txt', 'r') as f:  # Doesn't matter if file is SSD or network
    content = f.read()            # Same interface regardless of storage type

# ADT enables algorithm reuse — binary search works on ANY sorted sequence ADT
def binary_search(sequence, target):
    # Only uses ADT operations: len() and indexing []
    low, high = 0, len(sequence) - 1
    while low <= high:
        mid = (low + high) // 2
        if sequence[mid] == target: return mid
        elif sequence[mid] < target: low = mid + 1
        else: high = mid - 1
    return -1

# Works on list, tuple, array, any sequence ADT!
print(binary_search([1, 3, 5, 7, 9], 7))   # 3
print(binary_search((2, 4, 6, 8, 10), 6))  # 2

subtopics.title: Encapsulation in ADTs
subtopics.content:
Encapsulation is the property of bundling the data and the operations on that data into a single cohesive unit, while restricting external access to the internal state. In the context of ADTs, encapsulation means users interact with data exclusively through the defined operations — they cannot directly read or mutate the internal variables of the data structure.

In Python, encapsulation is implemented through the convention of name mangling and private attributes. Attributes prefixed with a single underscore (_attr) signal 'intended for internal use,' while double-underscore (__attr) triggers Python's name mangling to make direct external access practically impossible. Combined with Python's property decorators, this creates clean, encapsulated ADT implementations where the internal state is fully controlled.

The deeper reason encapsulation matters is the maintenance of invariants — conditions that must always remain true about the internal state. For a List ADT backed by an array, the invariant might be: 'self._size always equals the actual number of valid elements stored.' If users could directly modify self._size, they could create corrupt states that crash the program unpredictably. By routing all modifications through defined methods, invariants are enforced at every operation.

In modern industry, encapsulation is what makes large software systems manageable. When a team of 50 engineers works on a single codebase, the ADT boundary acts as a 'firewall' — engineers on the user side cannot accidentally break the implementation side and vice versa. This is why professional code review standards explicitly check for encapsulation violations.
examples:
# Real-world example: Python's heapq module encapsulates heap invariant
import heapq
heap = []
heapq.heappush(heap, 5)
heapq.heappush(heap, 2)
heapq.heappush(heap, 8)
print(heapq.heappop(heap))  # Always returns smallest: 2
# The heap property (parent <= children) is maintained internally
# You never manually rearrange the list — encapsulation enforces the invariant

subtopics.title: Information Hiding
subtopics.content:
Information hiding is a design principle formalized by David Parnas in 1972 that states: the implementation details most likely to change should be hidden behind a stable interface. While encapsulation is about bundling and access control, information hiding specifically focuses on shielding users from details they don't need and shouldn't depend on.

The practical consequence of information hiding is that the implementation can evolve freely — for performance improvements, bug fixes, or supporting new hardware — without affecting a single line of client code. If users could depend on implementation details (like knowing that a list is backed by a Python array), any change to that backing store would break all client code. By hiding those details, the implementer gains the freedom to optimize aggressively.

In Python, information hiding is achieved through a combination of conventions and language features: single underscore prefixes for internal-use attributes, double underscore for name-mangled private attributes, abstract base classes to define stable interfaces, and module-level __all__ lists to explicitly declare what is public. While Python doesn't enforce strict private access like Java or C++, the community strongly respects these conventions.

Information hiding is what makes large frameworks stable over years. Django's ORM, for example, hides whether it's using PostgreSQL, MySQL, or SQLite behind a uniform query interface. Applications written for Django work with any supported database without modification. This is the direct, practical result of information hiding at the framework level.
examples:
# Python's __all__ for module-level information hiding
# In mylist.py:
__all__ = ['List', 'create_list']  # Only these are public

class List:
    pass

def create_list():
    return List()

def _internal_helper():  # Hidden — not in __all__
    pass

# User imports:
# from mylist import *  → only gets List and create_list
# _internal_helper is hidden from the public interface

# Django ORM — classic information hiding example
# User code (depends only on stable interface):
# from django.db import models
# results = MyModel.objects.filter(age__gte=18).order_by('name')
# Django hides whether it generates PostgreSQL, MySQL, or SQLite SQL
# The same Python code works with any database backend!
# Switching databases = change one line in settings.py
# Zero changes to user code — that's information hiding in action

subtopics.title: ADT Operations: Constructors, Accessors, and Mutators
subtopics.content:
Operations defined on an ADT are classified into three fundamental categories based on their role and effect on the state: Constructors (creators), Accessors (observers), and Mutators (transformers). Understanding this classification is critical for designing correct, predictable ADTs and for building thread-safe and functional-style programs.

Constructors are operations that create and initialize a new instance of the ADT. In Python, this corresponds to the __init__ method or class-level factory functions like list(), dict(), or set(). Constructors establish the initial valid state of the ADT and ensure all internal invariants are satisfied from the very beginning. A well-designed constructor should never leave the object in an invalid or partially-initialized state.

Accessors (also called observers or queries) are operations that retrieve information from the ADT without modifying its state. Examples include size(), is_empty(), peek(), and get(index). Accessors are idempotent — calling them multiple times in a row always returns the same result (assuming no mutation in between). This property makes accessors safe for concurrent reads in multi-threaded systems, and it's why functional programming prefers pure accessor-like functions.

Mutators (also called transformers or commands) are operations that modify the internal state of the ADT. Examples include insert(), delete(), update(), push(), pop(), and clear(). Mutators change the state, so the result of an accessor called before a mutator may differ from one called after. Designing mutators carefully — ensuring they maintain all invariants and behave atomically (either fully succeed or fully fail) — is the essence of robust ADT implementation.
examples:
# Why the distinction matters: thread safety
import threading

# Accessors can safely run concurrently (read-only)
# Mutators need locks to prevent race conditions
class ThreadSafeStack:
    def __init__(self):
        self.__data = []
        self.__lock = threading.Lock()

    def peek(self):         # Accessor — read lock sufficient
        return self.__data[-1] if self.__data else None

    def is_empty(self):     # Accessor — read lock sufficient
        return len(self.__data) == 0

    def push(self, item):   # Mutator — needs exclusive write lock!
        with self.__lock:
            self.__data.append(item)

    def pop(self):          # Mutator — needs exclusive write lock!
        with self.__lock:
            return self.__data.pop()

# Demonstration of state changes by mutators
L = ListADT.from_iterable([10, 20, 30])
print(L.size())       # Accessor: 3
print(L.get(1))       # Accessor: 20

L.insert(1, 15)       # Mutator: [10, 15, 20, 30]
print(L.size())       # Accessor: 4 — changed!
print(L.get(1))       # Accessor: 15 — changed!

L.delete(0)           # Mutator: [15, 20, 30]
print(L.is_empty())   # Accessor: False
L.clear()             # Mutator: []
print(L.is_empty())   # Accessor: True

lessons.title: The List ADT
lessons.description: Deep dive into the List ADT — its definition, operations, and interface design.

subtopics.title: What is a List?
subtopics.content:
A List is one of the most fundamental Abstract Data Types in computer science. Formally, a List is an ordered, finite sequence of zero or more elements of the same type, where elements are accessed by their position (index). The critical term here is 'ordered' — this does not mean the elements are sorted by value, but that they occupy a definite sequence: there is a first element, a second, a third, and so on up to the last.

A list is classified as a linear data structure because its elements are logically arranged in a straight line. Each element (except the first) has exactly one predecessor, and each element (except the last) has exactly one successor. This strict linearity distinguishes lists from non-linear structures like trees (where one element can have many children) and graphs (where elements can have arbitrary connections).

The dynamic nature of a list is what makes it powerful: unlike a fixed-size array, a list can grow when elements are added and shrink when elements are removed. This dynamic behavior is a core part of the List ADT contract, even if the underlying implementation uses a fixed-capacity array with periodic resizing.

Lists are ubiquitous in every domain of computing. A music playlist, the history of visited URLs in a browser, the lines in a text file, the undo history in a document editor, the sequence of frames in a video — all of these are naturally modeled as List ADTs. Mastering the List ADT is the entry point into understanding how all sequential data in software is organized and manipulated.
examples:
# Python's built-in list is the most common List ADT implementation
playlist = ['Song A', 'Song B', 'Song C', 'Song D']
print(playlist[0])          # Access by position: 'Song A'
playlist.insert(2, 'Song X') # Insert at position 2
print(playlist)              # ['Song A', 'Song B', 'Song X', 'Song C', 'Song D']
playlist.remove('Song B')    # Delete by value
print(len(playlist))         # Size: 4

# Browser history as a List ADT
browser_history = []
browser_history.append('google.com')    # Visit page
browser_history.append('github.com')
browser_history.append('docs.python.org')

current = browser_history[-1]           # Current page
previous = browser_history[-2]          # Previous page
print(f'Current: {current}')            # docs.python.org
print(f'Back to: {previous}')           # github.com

browser_history.pop()                   # Press back button
print(browser_history[-1])              # Now at github.com

# Text editor undo history as a List ADT
undo_history = []
text = ''

def type_text(new_text):
    global text
    undo_history.append(text)  # Save current state
    text = new_text

def undo():
    global text
    if undo_history:
        text = undo_history.pop()  # Restore previous state

type_text('Hello')
type_text('Hello World')
type_text('Hello World!')
print(text)   # 'Hello World!'
undo()
print(text)   # 'Hello World'
undo()
print(text)   # 'Hello'

subtopics.title: List ADT Operations
subtopics.content:
The List ADT is characterized by a well-defined, minimal yet complete set of operations that together allow users to do everything meaningful with a list. These operations form the entire public interface — nothing inside the list is accessible except through these operations.

The core operations of the List ADT are: (1) insert(position, element) — inserts an element at a specified index, shifting subsequent elements one position to the right; (2) delete(position) — removes the element at a specified index, shifting subsequent elements one position to the left; (3) get(position) — returns the element at a given index without modifying the list; (4) set(position, element) — replaces the element at a given index with a new value; (5) find(element) — searches for an element and returns its first index, or -1 if not found; (6) size() — returns the number of elements currently in the list; (7) is_empty() — returns True if the list contains zero elements; (8) clear() — removes all elements, resetting the list to empty; and (9) print_list() — displays all elements in sequence.

Additional convenience operations commonly included are: append(element) as a special case of insert at the last position; prepend(element) as insert at position 0; first() and last() to access boundary elements; and slice(start, end) to retrieve a sublist.

Each operation has well-defined preconditions (conditions that must be true before calling) and postconditions (guaranteed state after the call). For instance, get(i) has the precondition: 0 <= i < size(). Violating preconditions typically results in an IndexError or equivalent exception.
examples:
# Operation complexity for a typical array-backed list
# insert(0, val)     → O(n)  — shift all elements right
# insert(n, val)     → O(1)  — add to end (amortized)
# delete(0)          → O(n)  — shift all elements left
# delete(n-1)        → O(1)  — remove from end
# get(i)             → O(1)  — direct index access
# find(val)          → O(n)  — must scan entire list
# size() / is_empty()→ O(1)  — stored counter
# clear()            → O(1)  — reset counter (or O(n) if freeing memory)

subtopics.title: List ADT Interface Design in Python
subtopics.content:
Designing a clean List ADT interface in Python means separating the 'what' (the interface/abstract class) from the 'how' (the concrete implementation). Python's abc (Abstract Base Classes) module provides the perfect tool for this: the ABC class and @abstractmethod decorator allow us to define a strict interface contract that any implementation must honor.

A well-designed ADT interface in Python consists of: an abstract base class that declares all operations as abstract methods, clear docstrings that specify the preconditions, postconditions, and error behavior of each operation, and a consistent naming convention that reflects the domain. The interface should be minimal — only include operations that cannot be derived from others. Extra convenience methods can be provided as concrete methods in the abstract class itself, implemented in terms of the abstract operations.

The power of this design pattern becomes evident when you have multiple concrete implementations. A ListADT abstract class can be subclassed by ArrayBasedList (using Python's list internally), LinkedList (using nodes), and SkipList (using probabilistic structure) — all honoring the same contract. Any code written to work with ListADT will work with all three implementations transparently.

This pattern is heavily used in Python's standard library and popular frameworks. Python's io module defines abstract base classes for streams (IOBase, RawIOBase, BufferedIOBase), allowing the same code to work with file streams, network streams, in-memory streams, and compressed streams. Django's backend interfaces, SQLAlchemy's connection abstractions, and NumPy's array protocol all follow this same ADT interface design.
examples:
# Polymorphism in action — same code works for any ListADT
def find_and_move_to_front(lst: ListADT, target):
    """Find target and move it to position 0 — works on any ListADT"""
    idx = lst.find(target)
    if idx == -1:
        return False
    element = lst.delete(idx)
    lst.insert(0, element)
    return True

# Works on ArrayList or any other ListADT implementation
my_list = ArrayList()
for v in [10, 20, 30, 40]:
    my_list.append(v)
print(my_list)                           # ArrayList([10, 20, 30, 40])
find_and_move_to_front(my_list, 30)
print(my_list)                           # ArrayList([30, 10, 20, 40])
